data.title: Olo: Strong Growth Justifies Thoma Bravo's $2B Buyout, But Upside Is Capped
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Olo Inc. (NYSE:OLO) has been on my radar for a while as a quietly growing player in restaurant technology, but the story has effectively been written for public investors. I first looked it up after covering PAR Technology in June, and the latter was even linked with acquiring it earlier this month. That’s not happening, of course.

The company delivered another strong quarter in Q2 2025, with double-digit revenue and ARPU growth, and management also reported continuous progress on margins. I think that those numbers partly explain why Thoma Bravo stepped in with an all-cash deal valuing Olo at about $2 billion in early July, which is a 65% premium to where the stock was trading at the time.

For current shareholders, the investment case now comes down to deal mechanics rather than fundamentals. Going with the current price, the upside is capped at the $10.25/share take-out price unless another bidder emerges. We had some reports of a slightly higher $13/share bid the same day the Thoma Bravo deal was announced, but that came to nothing. Clearly, there’s something to watch here, and I believe that the growth story still matters because it shows why Olo is attractive to private equity.

Q2 2025 Review

Olo’s Q2 2025 revenue grew 22% YoY to $85.7 million, which comfortably beat the high end of its own guidance. And the growth wasn’t one-dimensional: active locations are at 89,000, up from the ~88,000 reported in Q1 and 8.5% higher than the same period last year. At the same time, the ARPU rose 12% YoY to $955. That combination tells me adoption is broadening at the same time as existing customers are deepening their use of Olo’s suite. For a SaaS business, that’s exactly the mix you want.

The margins improved too, although not evenly everywhere. Gross profit grew 10% YoY to $43.9 million, though gross margin fell slightly due to higher hosting and service costs. Still, the company’s non-GAAP operating income jumped to $13.1 million, and that represents a 15% margin, so we’re seeing that leverage is starting to show through as volumes scale. I’m highlighting this because Olo generally pitches itself as a platform with meaningful incremental margins once penetration hits critical mass. In my view, Q2 gave a glimpse of that dynamic starting to play out.

Beyond the headline numbers, the product roadmap deserves attention. Take the company’s Digital Ordering Platform, for example. The product now integrates with Black Box Intelligence to offer what the latter calls “Unlock off-premise performance insights to empower frontline teams to drive improvements throughout the ordering experience.” That means that Olo can offer more customized solutions to its clients.

If we add that to the company’s growth in catering and loyalty, then Olo shifts from just order aggregation to a full restaurant operating system. In an industry like hospitality, loyalty drives stickier relationships, catering expands order value, and guest intelligence arms brands with data they can’t easily get elsewhere. I believe that Olo is forming a potentially defensible moat in plain sight, and it explains why investors had been warming back to the name even before the buyout news hit.

What’s The Upside of Thoma Bravo’s Privatization Deal?

On July 3, 2025, Olo announced that Thoma Bravo was going to acquire it in an all-cash deal valuing the company at around $2 billion. Shareholders are set to receive $10.25 per share, which represents a 65% premium to the stock’s pre-deal price. Deals at that kind of premium aren’t exactly commonplace in this market, especially for mid-cap SaaS, and I think it signals how much conviction Thoma Bravo has in Olo’s long-term runway.

From a shareholder standpoint, the story is now less about Olo’s operating performance and more about deal mechanics because at today’s price, the stock is essentially capped by the $10.25 offer.

For Thoma Bravo, however, I would say that the rationale is obvious. Olo has sticky recurring revenue, a long runway as restaurants digitize, and a model that’s just starting to show operating leverage. One can make an argument that the company is being taken private as a turnaround, but I’d argue it’s more of a business being taken private as it’s about to hit its stride.

The numbers speak for themselves: revenue up 22% year over year, ARPU climbing 12%, and product adoption broadening into areas like catering, loyalty, and guest intelligence. That mix is a sign that Olo is embedding itself deeper into restaurant operations, and this makes it harder to rip out and easier to expand. This is the kind of stickiness that private equity firms love because it supports both recurring revenue and pricing power over time.

We should also consider the broader market context. Restaurants are still trying to modernize their digital ordering and guest engagement platforms, and Olo is one of the few scaled vendors with a purpose-built solution. Yes, the company has competitors like PAR Technology, but it also has the benefit of a large installed base. Add that to its improving ability to drive more revenue from those locations year after year, and we have the makings of a company heading in the right direction.

When Olo goes into private hands, the ownership allows for the company to make heavier investments in new modules like analytics, guest engagement, and payments without the constant quarterly scrutiny from analysts and public shareholders like you and me. One can also expect the usual cost reduction and efficiency measures that come with such a takeover. I believe that if properly managed, Thoma Bravo buying a platform with both scale and optionality when the broader SaaS market is still trading at more modest multiples gives them major upside in the case of an exit in the medium term.

I think the irony here is that Olo is leaving the public market just as the signs point to its fundamentals starting to align with the SaaS story it pitched at IPO. All the same, I don’t think shareholders will be complaining. After all, they’ll get certainty and a tidy premium. Still, the next chapter, the one where Olo builds out a full-stack platform under PE ownership, won’t play out on public screens.

Takeaway

Olo’s recent results paint the picture of a business that is going to hit its stride sooner than later, and the fundamentals explain why it’s being privatized. For public investors, the ceiling is set at $10.25 per share, and any upside beyond that rests entirely on the extremely unlikely chance of a competing bid coming in before the deal closes at the end of 2025. That’s why I see Olo as essentially a “wait-and-close” story at this point. Shareholders get certainty and a nice premium, and that’s it.

data.title: QQCC:CA : A Defensive Growth Income Play That Outshines QYLD
data.description:
The Global X NASDAQ-100 Covered Call ETF (TSX:QQCC:CA) is the unleveraged version of QQCL:CA, that I recently wrote about. It is also Canada's version of QYLD with currency exposure. Performance data shows QQCC:CA below QQCL:CA in capturing growth, but slightly better in drawdown management. It is also way above QYLD in terms of total returns. That boils down to structural differences which generate significant alpha and position QQCC:CA as a Buy for defensive growth income seekers.

Methodology - Pros and Cons

The methodology is broadly in line with most other option writing income ETFs i.e. the underlying (in this case NDX) is replicated as a core holding (in this case through an ETF holding i.e. Global X Nasdaq ETF). Call options are written on top of the core holdings to generate income.

Usually this methodology has several deficiencies like the well known upside capping impact. Drawdowns therefore are limited, but not contained beyond a point. Over the long term, this leads to relatively high risk while returns (in total return terms) remain muted.

QQCC:CA makes adjustments to the usual option writing approach by making three critical adjustments that ensure overall income is more sustainable in the long term. One, it writes options on up to 50% of the portfolio. This means upside movements are captured to a great extent. Two, the extent of option writing appears to be discretionary. So while the core holding is still passive, the option generating layer is truly an actively managed layer. This enables even full upside capture if execution backs up the potential because QQCC:CA can be flexible about the amount of coverage the option layer provides. Three, the options are written out of the money, typically for expiries that are 1-2 months out from the time of taking fresh covered writing positions. This also enables higher upside capture while lowering the option premium earned.

The tweaks appear minor, but its effects are far reaching. I have highlighted how the bulk of the income from covered call strategies come from the capital appreciation of the underlying in reality, in various past analyses on such ETFs. QYLD like ETFs write options typically on 100% of the portfolio and at strikes aggressively close to the money (at the money). This theoretically does better during corrections. But over the long term generate very little alpha that can be returned to investors as income. The drawdown benefits of a few percentage points over a QQCC:CA like strategy are far lower than the opportunity loss of 20-30 percentage points on the upside.

Fact is option premiums end up contributing far too less in generating income over various market cycles and by aggressively trying to maximize that, QYLD like option heavy strategies actually kill the overall income generated. Result is often a declining NAV over time and yields, even though apparently sustained, keep diminishing in absolute terms. QQCC:CA does the opposite. It allows the primary income lever, capital appreciation to play out better. Option income is a relatively minor enhancement to the total returns. By aiming for lower option income, it actually captures that small but meaningful alpha more often than not.

The below comparison of yields between the more aggressive QYLD and QQCC:CA shows the differences narrowing in recent times. A close to 10% yield is anyway a great yield in absolute terms. Any higher yield is too aggressive and dampens the NAV even further. Which means lower capital on the table for future dividends, even at same yield levels.

The graph below, depicting the share prices, shows how the combination of a less aggressive option strategy and a more conservative payout policy allows QQCC:CA to grow and deliver more absolute returns over time, while QYLD structurally weathers down. This comparison does not assume reinvestments, unlike a total return comparison, which is more likely for the case for income seekers.

Performance Benefits

A comparison of QQCC:CA against the leveraged QQCL:CA, QYLD and QQQ (a proxy for the underlying NDX holding) show the expected performance outcomes. In over one and a half years, QYLD is visibly not growing sufficiently and that includes the impact of payouts (i.e. total returns and that too if reinvested).

That performance difference compared with the Canadian counterparts could also be often dictated by favorable currency fluctuations. However in the period of analysis, the Canadian dollar has actually weakened by ~4% and has never seen any improvement beyond 5% levels against the USD, highlighting how the methodology is purely driving the outperformance.

Of course, the outperformance is aided by a supportive bull rally that QQCC:CA was able to capture during the period. The comparison below shows relative total returns during the tariff sell off in 2025 lasting from February to April. QQCL:CA's higher leverage leads to higher drawdowns, but not significantly so, as I have noted in my thesis for QQCL:CA. QYLD does provide a little better drawdown cushion because of its aggressive option positioning, but only marginally so. A one and a half percentage point drawdown cushion is hardly any benefit in lieu of a 25 percentage point upside difference we have seen earlier. QQCC:CA may even double down on option writing during stress scenarios due to its active style to reduce the drawdowns (although the 50% guideline implies lower protection than QYLD). Overall, QQCC:CA does provide drawdown protection better than QQQ and almost similar to QYLD. This is a result of active management of positions and the overall low impact of written calls to mitigate drawdowns as I had highlighted earlier (which doesn't help QYLD much as seen).

Case for a Strong Buy

I had rated QQCL:CA a Buy because its leverage risks work out reasonably well and the growth profile is far superior. QQCC:CA does even better on drawdowns and in hypothetical deeper and prolonged crashes, it will definitely be safer than QQCL:CA's leveraged approach. The upside capture in QQCC:CA is reasonable, although will surely lag QQCL:CA.

The expense ratio of 0.79% is also much lower than QQCL:CA's 0.85%, although that is a minor impact in the overall scheme of things. The expense ratio is on an absolute scale towards the higher side. That is expected given the active management and option trading layer. At least QQCC:CA delivers far higher value against those propositions compared to QYLD (expense ratio 0.6%) for a higher expense ratio.

QQCL:CA is still a Buy for more growth oriented investors because its risk profile is suitable for the higher returns it generates. QQCC:CA though is a Strong Buy because of the stronger risk management due to lack of leverage. This means its risk adjusted returns are similar to QQCL:CA or only slightly poorer. For an income plan, total returns is not the primary objective - sustainability of income over time and across market conditions is. Hence I will prefer QQCC:CA for income focused investors. In periods of stagnation or corrections, you may find QQCC:CA more sustainable, while its upside capture is also close to QQCL:CA's higher growth promise.

data.title: Monthly Report: Precious Metals Royalty And Streaming Companies - July 2025
data.description:
Precious metals royalty and streaming companies represent a very interesting sub-industry of the precious metals mining industry. They provide some leverage to the growing metals prices, similar to the typical mining companies; however, they are less risky in comparison to them. Their incomes are derived from royalty and streaming agreements. Under a metal streaming agreement, the streaming company provides an upfront payment to acquire the right to future deliveries of a predefined percentage of the metal production of a mining operation.

The streaming company also pays some ongoing payments that are usually well below the market price of the metal. They can be set as a fixed sum (e.g., $300/toz gold) or as a percentage (e.g., 20% of the prevailing gold price), or a combination of both (e.g., the lower of a) $300/toz gold and b) 20% of the prevailing gold price). The royalties usually apply to a small fraction of the mining project production (usually 1-3%), and they are not connected with ongoing payments. They can have various forms, but the most common is a small percentage of the net smelter return ("NSR"). The NSR is calculated as revenues from the sale of the mined products minus transportation and refining costs.

To better track the overall performance of the whole sub-industry, I created a capitalization-weighted index (the Precious Metals Royalty and Streaming Index) consisting of 11 companies (in June 2020, expanded to 15). Later, based on the inquiries of readers, I also introduced an equal-weighted version of the index. Until March 2021, both indices included the same companies and were calculated back to January 2019.

However, some major changes occurred in April 2021. Due to the boom of the royalty and streaming industry and the emergence of many new companies, the indices experienced two major changes. First of all, the market capitalization-weighted index was modified to include only the 5 biggest companies: Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), Royal Gold (RGLD), Osisko Gold Royalties (OR), and Sandstorm Gold (SAND). The combined weight of these 5 companies on the old index was around 95%, therefore, the small companies had only a negligible impact on their performance. The values of the index were re-calculated back to January 2019, and between January 2019 and March 2021, the difference in the overall performance of the old and the new index was only 2.29 percentage points. The second change is related to the equally weighted index that was expanded to 20 companies.

The previous editions of the monthly report can be found here: May 2019, June 2019, July 2019, August 2019, September 2019, October 2019, November 2019, December 2019, January 2020, February 2020, March 2020, April 2020, May 2020, June 2020, July 2020, August 2020, September 2020, October 2020, November 2020, December 2020, January 2021, February 2021, March 2021, April 2021, May 2021, June 2021, July 2021, August 2021, September 2021 (extended), October 2021 (extended), November 2021 (extended), December 2021 (extended), January 2022 (extended), February 2022 (extended), March 2022 (extended), April 2022 (extended), May 2022 (extended), June 2022 (extended), July 2022 (extended), August 2022 (extended), September 2022 (extended), October 2022 (extended), November 2022 (extended), December 2022 (extended), January 2023 (extended), February 2023 (extended), March 2023 (extended), April 2023 (extended), May 2023 (extended), June 2023 (extended), July 2023 (extended), August 2023 (extended), September 2023 (extended), October 2023 (extended), November 2023 (extended), December 2023 (extended), January 2024 (extended), February 2024 (extended), March 2024 (extended), April 2024 (extended), May 2024 (extended), June 2024 (extended), July 2024 (extended), August 2024 (extended), September 2024 (extended), October 2024 (extended), November 2024 (extended), December 2024 (extended), January 2025 (extended), February 2025 (extended), March 2025 (extended), April 2025 (extended), May 2025 (extended), June 2025 (extended).

As of the end of July, Wheaton's market capitalization stood at $41.5 billion. Wheaton was followed by Franco-Nevada ($30.7 billion), and Royal Gold ($10 billion). At the bottom of the ranking was positioned Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF) with a market capitalization of less than $13.4 million. The most important change that occurred in July is the end of the original Orogen Royalties, which was acquired by Triple Flag. However, Triple Flag only extracted the 1% NSR royalty on the Expanded Silicon Gold Project, and all the remaining assets were left in a spin-out company (new Orogen), which was re-listed on TSX-V. So Orogen will return to the list as soon as the dust settles down.

In general, July was a good month for the precious metals R&S companies. Out of 16 companies, 10 ended in green numbers, one remained flat, and five recorded a share price decline. The highest gains, more than 31%, recorded Empress Royalty (OTCQX:EMPYF). The growth was supported by a new royalty acquisition. Nations Royalty (NRC:CA) experienced a nearly 30% share price growth, however, there was no company-specific news that could explain it. On the other hand, the biggest decline, by nearly 15%, experienced Royal Gold. The market was not too happy about the acquisition of Sandstorm Gold and Horizon Copper. However, at least Sandstorm was acquired at a price favorable for Royal Gold.

In July, the precious metals prices didn't move a lot. The share price of the SPDR Gold Trust ETF (GLD) declined by 0.61%, and the share price of the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) increased by 1.55%. Similarly, the share price of the VanEck Vectors Gold Miners ETF (GDX) declined only by 0.81%. On the other hand, the smaller miners did worse, and the VanEck Vectors Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) declined by 4.99%. The Precious Metals R&S Index declined by 1.55%, as the big players didn't do too well, moreover, Royal Gold experienced a steep decline. On the other hand, the Precious Metals R&S Equally Weighted Index grew by 6.64%, as some of the smaller players did really well.

The July News

This time, July hasn't been a boring month, thanks to Royal Gold, which decided to shake up the structure of the whole industry, as it announced the acquisition of Sandstorm Gold and Horizon Copper (and in August, it also added a $1 billion investment in a new world-class gold stream). But a big investment was also announced by Franco-Nevada.

Franco-Nevada (FNV) announced the acquisition of a 1% NSR royalty on AngloGold Ashanti's (AU) Arthur Gold Project (formerly known as Expanded Silicon Project) from Altius Minerals (OTCPK:ATUSF) for $250 million in cash and contingent payments of $25 million. Arthur contains indicated and inferred resources of 16.3 million toz gold, and it keeps on growing.

Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) declared the second edition of the $1 Million Future of Mining Challenge. The 2025/26 challenge will focus on sustainable water management in the mining industry.

Royal Gold (RGLD) announced the acquisition of Sandstorm Gold (SAND) and Horizon Copper (OTCQX:HNCUF). Shareholders of Sandstorm Gold will receive 0.0625 shares of Royal Gold for each share of Sandstorm Gold. As of the day of announcement (July 7), this represented a premium of 21% based on the 20-day volume-weighted average price, and a premium 17% based on the closing price of Sandstorm shares prior to the deal announcement. The deal valued Sandstorm at approximately $3.5 billion. The Horizon Copper shareholders will receive C$2 per share, which values Horizon Copper at nearly C$200 million. The acquisition price represents a premium of 85% to the 20-day volume-weighted average price, and 72% to the pre-announcement closing price. The two acquisitions should increase Royal Gold's attributable gold equivalent production by approximately 25%, and further boost its growth potential. Also the diversification, in terms of operational risks and jurisdiction risks, will increase. The combined company will have 80 cash-flowing assets, with no asset accounting for more than 13% of NAV, and 47 development-stage assets. There will also be an additional 266 assets in earlier exploration stages.

On July 8, Royal Gold announced preliminary Q2 sales of 40,600 toz of gold equivalent (32,200 toz gold, 578,700 toz silver, and 1,100 tonnes copper).

OR Royalties (OR) reported attributable Q2 gold equivalent production of 19,700 toz. The preliminary revenues amounted to $60.4 million. As of the end of Q2, the company held cash of $49.6 million.

Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM) sold 28,682 toz of gold equivalent (67.6% gold, 32.4% silver) in Q2. The preliminary revenues amounted to $94.1 million. The company also completed the acquisition of Orogen Royalties.

Sandstorm Gold (SAND) announced that it had agreed to be acquired by Royal Gold (see news above). The company also announced that it sold approximately 15,100 toz of gold equivalent in Q2, generating preliminary revenues of $51.4 million.

Gold Royalty (GROY) announced that in Q2, it recorded revenues, land agreement proceeds, and interests of $4.4 million, which is approximately 100% more than in the same period of last year. The sales amounted to 1,346 toz of gold equivalent. The company reiterated its 2025 guidance of 5,700-7,000 toz of gold equivalent.

EMX Royalty (EMX) announced the sale of its Moroccan asset portfolio and the creation of an exploration alliance with Avesoro Morocco LTD. Avesoro paid $650,000 for the asset portfolio. EMX is also entitled to some contingency payments. Moreover, it will provide a capital pool of $1.5 million per year over the next two years for exploration of the Moroccan projects that are a part of the alliance. Avesoro has the option to deem any of the projects a Designated Project (Avesoro will retain 100% ownership and EMX a 2% NSR royalty). All the projects not converted into a Designated Project will return to EMX.

On July 24, EMX announced that the Vittangi graphite mine in Sweden had obtained all the major permits necessary to start production. EMX holds a 2% NSR royalty over Vittangi.

Vox Royalty (VOXR) announced that Norton Gold Fields received permission to expand the throughput of the Binduli North mine by 40%, to 7 Mtpa. At this run rate, Vox's Janet Ivy royalty should be generating cash flows of around $2 million per year.

Sailfish Royalty (OTCQX:SROYF) announced that Solidus Resources' Spring Valley gold mine is fully permitted now. Sailfish holds a 3% NSR royalty over the majority of the proposed open pit, and 0.5%-1.5% NSR royalty over its remainder.

Orogen Royalties (OGN:CA) completed the Plan of Arrangement with Triple Flag, based on which the company was acquired for $171.5 million in cash, $171.5 million in Triple Flag shares, and $78 million in implied value of shares of New Orogen. New Orogen holds all the assets of original Orogen, with the exception of the 1% NSR royalty on the Expanded Silicon Gold Project. The shares of the new company were listed on TSXV as of July 11.

Nations Royalty (NRC:CA) granted to certain directors, officers, employees, and consultants options to purchase 1,015,000 shares of the company in total, at an exercise price of C$0.58 per share. The options have a 5-year maturity.

Elemental Altus Royalties (OTCQX:ELEMF) announced that Capricorn Metals (OTCPK:CRNLF) received permission for the full development of the Karlawinda Expansion Project. The expansion should increase the production at the mine by 25%, to 125,000 toz gold per year. The expansion should be completed by the end of 2026. Elemental holds a 2% NSR royalty over Karlawinda.

Empress Royalty (OTCQX:EMPYF) announced the acquisition of a 5% royalty on gold and silver produced at the Milford Copper mine in Utah. Empress paid $3 million to Milford Mining Company for the royalty. The company will use the proceeds to expand the mine. The royalty will terminate after Empress receives payments of $12 million in total.

Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF) announced that Green Star Royalties was named a 2025 Carbon Positive Awards winner by Green Cross United Kingdom.

Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA) announced the closing of the final tranche of its non-brokered offering of units, announced in May. The final tranche consisted of 132,693 units priced at C$6.5.

The August Outlook

The month of August entered its second half, and for now, it has been successful for the precious metals R&S industry. If the gold price as well as the broader stock market avoid a deeper correction, the positive tone should also prevail over the remainder of the month.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHY Provides Exposure To International, Dividend-Paying Stocks
data.description:
The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHY) is a passively managed fund designed to track the Dow Jones International Dividend 100 Index. SCHY is a low-cost, income-oriented strategy that provides investors with international exposure. SCHY has a 3.72% dividend yield on a trailing twelve-month basis and has an expense ratio of 8bps.

About Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF

SCHY was launched on April 29, 2021, by Charles Schwab as a passively managed investment vehicle designed to provide investors with exposure to dividend-paying international stocks. The strategy has a low management fee of 8bps. The strategy has roughly $1.3b in assets under management (AUM) with an average of 364k shares changing hands on a daily basis with minimal spread risk, or the cost-in/cost-out, for trading shares. SCHY is benchmarked to the underlying index, the Dow Jones International Dividend 100 Index.

Index Structure

SCHY is indexed to the Dow Jones International Dividend 100 Index. The Index includes international ex-US dividend-paying mid- and large-cap companies while excluding real estate investment trusts (REITs). The stock screening process includes:

  • Minimum of 10 consecutive years of dividend payments.
  • Minimum float-adjusted market capitalization (FCM) of $500mm.
  • Minimum 6-month median daily value traded (MDVT) of $2mm.
  • Non-constituents must be domiciled in a country whose universe weight is greater than or equal to 0.20%.
  • Dividend yield must be equal to or greater than the median-indicated dividend yield of the eligible universe or the 40th percentile of the universe for the existing constituents.

Following these screening steps, equities are then ranked based on fundamental qualities, such as free cash flow as a percentage of total debt, return on equity, the indicated dividend yield, and the 5-year dividend growth rate. From there, the investment universe will be further reduced based on stock volatility.

Stocks are weighted quarterly and can make up no more than 4% of the index; sector representation can be no more than 15% of the Index. Emerging market exposure is capped at 15%.

Portfolio Construction

SCHY is fully invested in international equities across 103 holdings, the majority of which are mid- to large-cap stocks. The top-weighted country exposure includes the UK at 15.36%, Australia at 12%, and France at 11.65%. Industry exposure is heavily weighted to financials and consumer staples, making up 15% and 14.84% of the total portfolio weight, respectively.

The top 10 holdings make up 40% of the total portfolio weight, while the bottom 10 make up 0.75% of the portfolio weight. Top companies within the strategy include British American Tobacco (BTI) at 4.76%, Wesfarmers (OTCPK:WFAFF) at 4.21%, and BHP Group (BHP) at 4.17%. British American Tobacco is known for its cigarette brands, including Camel, Newport, and Natural American Spirit. Wesfarmers is an outdoor living and building materials retailer, similar to Fleet Farm in the US Midwest. BHP is an international mining company and is a primary supplier of copper, iron ore, and coal.

SCHY pays out an appealing quarterly distribution rate of $1.05/share, yielding 3.72% on a trailing twelve-month basis. SCHY transitioned from semiannual distributions to quarterly distributions in 2024. Comparing annualized distributions, SCHY has exhibited annual growth since inception.

How to Invest in SCHY

SCHY can be used as a passive income strategy with international exposure. Given that this is an indexed investment vehicle, investors will be responsible for managing this holding as part of a portfolio strategy. This may require some tactical positioning by the investor in order to manage risk. Distributions may come in the form of capital gains and dividend income, making SCHY a good investment candidate for tax-deferred accounts like an IRA or 401(k). For advisors, SCHY can provide international exposure across market sectors.

Compared to peer strategies, SCHY is the lowest cost strategy. SCHY is one of the larger portfolio strategies with $1.3b in AUM while providing substantially more depth for active trading. Despite these factors, SCHY has underperformed peer international dividend income strategies across historical periods on a total return basis.

The top-performing peer is the Global X MSCI SuperDividend EAFE ETF (EFAS), outperforming peers across most historical periods. Despite the appealing performance, EFAS has a mere $30mm in AUM and more restrictive liquidity. EFAS may be seen as a relatively more diversified strategy, with the top 10 holdings making up only 22% of the total portfolio weight despite only holding 58 stocks.

Risks Related to SCHY

Index funds are not actively managed, meaning that they are designed to reflect the underlying Index, providing investors with passive performance. As a result, it is left to the investors to actively manage their positions and risk in order to achieve their investment mandate. This compares to an actively managed strategy where professional portfolio managers aim to beat their stated benchmark by actively managing portfolio risk, whether it’s through stock selection or active weights.

SCHY may expose investors to country risk, including geopolitical, economic, and exchange rate and interest rate risk. SCHY may also be exposed to some liquidity risk relating to foreign investments.

Final Thoughts

SCHY can provide investors with low-cost exposure to dividend-paying international stocks. Given that this is an indexed strategy, investors should consider tactical positioning as the strategy is not actively managed and shouldn’t be expected to deliver optimized performance.

This article answers these three main questions about SCHY:

  • Is SCHY actively or passively managed?
  • Does SCHY provide investors with dividend income from international stocks?
  • Are there specific risks associated with SCHY and international investments?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Bilibili: Active Users Keep Surging
data.description:
Right now, diversification is more important than anything. With the stock market's gains being primarily driven by large-cap U.S. tech stocks, it's important to rotate more of our portfolios away from the recent winners and into more under-recognized small/mid-cap growth stocks, as well as international stocks.

Bilibili (NASDAQ:BILI) hits both of those birds with one stone. An entertainment platform that is frequently thought of as the "YouTube of China," Bilibili has substantially expanded its appeal in recent years, adding new social commerce and gaming features that have drawn in a large pool of new users, all of whom are spending record time on the app. Recently, Bilibili posted strong Q2 results that showcased yet another surge in users, but the stock fell ~5% as a result. Still, this hiccup doesn't break Bilibili's momentum. The stock is up ~40% since the start of January, and in my view, the company has far further to rally.

I last wrote a buy on Bilibili in July, when the stock was trading at similar ~$23 levels. Since then, the company has continued to add new daily active users while time spent on Bilibili continues to rise, fostering a double-digit surge in advertising revenue. At the same time, the company isn't just purely about growth, either: recent moves to capitalize on economies of scale have also meaningfully lifted the company's profit margins. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my buy rating on this company.

To me, these are the core reasons to be bullish on Bilibili:

  • Many categories under one roof. Bilibili offers a lot under its umbrella: from traditional video to short-form streaming, social commerce, and mobile gaming. The diversity of its offerings has helped the company draw in a large base of users, which is now topping 100 million in DAUs (daily active users).
  • Massive growth in gaming. In particular, gaming has been a significant growth driver for Bilibili, especially with its in-house developed hit San Mou. Gaming revenue is surging over 60% y/y while also drawing in a wide audience for game streaming content.
  • Tertiary revenue opportunities. Bilibili also has a lot of undersung services under its "value-added services" segment that further illustrate the diversity of its offerings. Smaller monetization streams include pay-to-view comics, a crafts marketplace (making Bilibili the equivalent of China's Etsy (ETSY) as well), and paid educational course content.
  • Chinese consumers are gravitating toward local content. The Chinese market is increasingly turning away from American entertainment (especially as Hollywood output slows and tensions between the U.S. and China flare) and more toward local Chinese content, which is driving larger audiences for Bilibili's network.
  • Economies of scale. As Bilibili's audience and revenue grow, it isn't increasing corporate opex at the same rate as revenue, allowing the company to start meaningfully expanding its operating margin profile.

Stay long here: in my view, Bilibili's momentum has a lot of steam post-earnings.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Bilibili's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Bilibili's revenue grew 20% y/y to RMB 7.34 billion, ahead of consensus expectations of 7.33 billion. The company's revenue growth did, however, decelerate four points versus 24% y/y growth in Q1, but against the backdrop of a tighter global macroeconomy, especially in the wake of China's trade spat with the U.S., Bilibili's ability to maintain double-digit growth is impressive.

Equally heartening is the company's consistent addition of DAUs to its base sequentially versus Q1. The company added 2 million net-new DAUs to end the quarter with 109 million daily users, while hitting a Q2 record for an average of 105 million minutes spent per user on the Bilibili platform.

Growth in engagement, via both an increase in users as well as time on the platform, has helped the company grow its advertising revenue by 20% y/y to $2.04 billion, which was in line with Q1's 20% y/y growth pace. Again, the company's ability to maintain its advertising growth rate in a tough macroeconomy is a strong signal of Bilibili's early-stage appeal as it broadens to more Chinese consumers.

Per CEO Rui Chen's remarks on the Q2 earnings call detailing user engagement and content trends:

Driven by high-quality PUGV content, the average daily time spent reached 105 minutes in the second quarter, up 6 minutes year-over-year. Notably, the watch time for videos longer than 5 minutes increased nearly 20% year-over-year in Q2, proving Bilibili's undeniable user mind share to consume mid- to long-form meaningful content. Not only are people spending more time, but more people are paying for premium content.

Monthly paying users increased by 9% year-over-year to 31 million, driven by membership, fan charging, and live broadcasting. In the meantime, our community remains engaged and loyal. The number of official members was up by 11% year-over-year to 270 million. [...]

Game content remained one of our top categories with watch time increasing by 21% year-over-year, making Bilibili an essential platform for game developers to engage deeply with the gamer community and strengthen their IP. It is also no surprise that AI-related content continues to be a breakout category. Watch time grew 61% year-over-year, cementing Bilibili's role as the go-to platform for Gen Z+ to learn, explore, and create with AI."

Where we do have to be careful, however, is the slight deceleration in gaming revenue. Gaming is still generating 60% y/y revenue growth and represents a record 22% of the company's revenue; however, sequentially, gaming revenue has declined each quarter since 24Q3.

Gaming is driven by hits, particularly San Mou. There are potential mitigating factors here, however. The company notes that it is preparing to launch San Mou internationally this year. On top of this, the company also has a number of games in the development pipeline (which it describes as several anime/comics games and a number of casual games) for the Chinese market. Hopefully, the increased depth and breadth of its content will help to keep the game's growth engine humming.

On the profitability front, we note the fact that Bilibili has managed to shrink operating expenses to 29% of revenue, from 35% in the year-ago quarter. This was particularly driven by a 3-point reduction in both sales/marketing costs as well as R&D costs, as the company's revenue growth (particularly in gaming) drives economies of scale.

Bilibili isn't just a growth story anymore, in other words. Pro forma operating margins leaped to 8%, up 13 points from -5% in the year-ago Q2. The company's pro forma EPS of RMB 1.29 also beat the consensus' 1.20 expectations with 8% upside.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

Despite Bilibili's hyper-growth, we continue to appreciate the fact that, at least on a revenue basis, Bilibili remains on the lower end against a number of both Chinese and U.S. internet peers:

In my view, Snap (SNAP) is particularly compelling in the U.S., since both platforms generate high-margin advertising revenue from a video streaming platform. Snap has recently run into challenges growing its DAU base in the U.S.—and yet, the company is still trading at a ~10% richer revenue multiple than Bilibili, which is having no problem growing engagement as well as several different revenue streams.

Of course, we should be wary of the risks that Bilibili faces. Streaming and social media are fad-driven, as are games. Bilibili constantly needs to stay on top of trends and release new gaming content in order to stay relevant and hold onto its user base. On top of this, the global macroeconomy (least of all the Chinese economy) remains on shaky footing with weak consumer spending, which may threaten demand from advertisers.

So far, however, we've only seen very strong execution from this company, and with the stock still trading at modest revenue multiples relative to other Internet peers, we see plenty of room for Bilibili to keep rallying.

data.title: Truist Financial: The Preferred Dividend Enjoys Excellent Coverage
data.description:
Introduction

Truist Financial (NYSE:TFC) is the holding company that owns Truist Bank, the oldest bank in North Carolina. The bank continued to grow in the past 150 years and is now a top-10 commercial bank in the US, servicing its customers with its network of almost 2,000 branches. As it had been a while since I had a look at its preferred shares, I wanted to see if the 6%-plus-yielding preferred stock is a good opportunity for fixed-income investors.

The bank’s results are good, and the preferred dividends are well covered

It goes without saying that one of the most important elements when looking at a preferred stock is to see how well those preferred dividends are covered.

Looking at the income statement provided by Truist Financial, we see the bank reported a total interest income of approximately $6.15B, a decrease of roughly $200M while the total amount of interest expenses decreased by just over $250M. The combination of both resulted in a net interest income increase of approximately $60 million, which represents just under 2%.

As the income statement below also shows, the bank reported a total non-interest income of $1.4B, while the total amount of non-interest expenses came in at just under $3B, including a $28 million restructuring expense.

Looking at the bottom line result, these elements add up to a pre-tax income of approximately $2B before taking the $488M in loan loss provisions into account. The reported net profit was roughly $1.24B, of which $1.18B was attributable to the common shareholders of the bank. This resulted in an EPS of $0.91, while the total earnings per share in the first semester came in at $1.80.

As the income statement above also shows, the bank needed less than 5% of its net profit to cover the preferred dividends in the second quarter. And looking at the more realistic H1 payout ratio (some preferred shares don’t have a quarterly payment schedule), we see that about 7% of the net income (which includes almost $1B in loan loss provisions and $66M in restructuring charges) was used to cover the preferred dividends.

The financial results also indicate that dividends on the common shares are quite well covered. The bank currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.52, which means that the payout ratio in the first half of this year was just under 60%. The dividend hasn't increased for a while now, so perhaps the strong financial results may also result in a dividend hike as the consensus estimates seem to be projecting an annualized dividend of $2.2 for FY 2027.

Another look at the Series R preferred shares

About 1.5 years ago, I focused on the bank’s Series R preferred shares, which are trading with (TFC.PR.R) as a ticker symbol. This series pays a fixed preferred dividend of 4.75% of its principal value of $25. Given the low cost of capital (4.75% for equity is pretty cheap), I doubt these securities will be called anytime soon (the first potential call date is in September). As such, I don’t think it's relevant to highlight the yield-to-call simply because a call appears to be unlikely (and even if they were called this year or next year, I’d be ecstatic to lock in a 28% capital gain).

Considering that the preferred shares are trading at a discount to the principal value of $25 per share, the current yield is approximately 6.1%. And given the strong result of the bank, I think the risk/reward ratio is pretty attractive.

Investment thesis

I currently have no position in any of the securities mentioned in this article, but as I'm looking to deploy more cash in fixed-income securities, I'm giving the preferred shares issued by Truist Financial another really good look. Additionally, I'm also not ruling out a long position in the common shares of the bank, given its interesting growth profile over the next few years, as it wants to open a hundred additional branches. These expansion plans also lend more credibility to the consensus estimates, which are pointing toward a 10% earnings growth in both 2026 and 2027.

If the consensus estimates are somewhat correct, the common shares of the bank are currently trading at just nine times the forward earnings for 2027, and that makes it an intriguing opportunity. I also noticed there are options available on Truist Financial, and I may consider writing some out-of-the-money put options.

data.title: Verb Technology: Better To Buy Toncoin Than VERB Stock
data.description:
Verb Technology Company Inc. (NASDAQ:VERB) has undergone a dramatic transformation in recent years. Originally positioned as a SaaS provider for interactive video-based sales tools, the company has pivoted into the fields of social commerce and digital asset management. The cause for the recent stock price movement is the decision to initiate a corporate treasury of Toncoin, the native cryptocurrency of The Open Network (TON) blockchain.

This has become much more common for companies to hold crypto on the balance sheet. This is even more than just buying crypto to store in the Treasury. The company recently announced that its treasury assets exceed $713 million of TON, which is more than half of its market cap. The company is also planning to rebrand as Ton Strategy Company. It is not merely adding crypto to the Treasury holdings, but more so that the company intends to put focus on crypto as a business.

My question becomes, is there any value in the underlying business, or is all the value in the holding of TON? If an investor is going to buy into the business just to hold TON, then why not go buy and hold TON directly? Is the underlying business going to hold back or increase the value of the Treasury holdings?

Company Overview

Verb operates at the intersection of livestream shopping, AI-powered video engagement, and most recently, blockchain-based treasury strategies. The company operates in three business units: MARKET.live, Go Fund Yourself (a TV show), and Vanity Prescribed/Good Girl Rx.

MARKET.live is an omnichannel livestream shopping marketplace designed to capitalize on the growth of influencer-led retail and video commerce. In April 2025, Verb expanded its reach through the acquisition of Lyvecom, an AI-driven, no-code platform that allows creators and brands to convert videos into shoppable content. Lyvecom also introduced automated virtual hosts and repurposing tools that significantly lower the barrier to entry for merchants and influencers seeking to scale video-driven e-commerce. This acquisition dovetails with MARKET.live, creating a more robust, integrated ecosystem.

Go Fund Yourself is a platform combines an interactive reality TV show that has been described as a combination of Shark Tank and Kickstarter with MARKET.live’s back-end capabilities allowing viewers to tap or scan onscreen icons and QR codes to facilitate an investment, in near real time, as they watch companies presenting before the Show’s panel of “Titans”. Presenting companies that sell consumer products are able to offer their products directly to viewers during the show in near real time through the same onscreen technology. (company filings)

Vanity Prescribed/Good Girl Rx is a telehealth initiative that operates in the rapid growth associated with the resale of the new weight-loss drugs. Vanity Prescribed leverages MARKET.live’s social commerce technology, which the Company intends to employ to disrupt the traditional healthcare model by utilizing social commerce capabilities to provide tailored healthcare solutions at affordable, fixed prices, without hidden fees, membership costs, or inflated pharmaceutical markups. (company filings)

The biggest move that the company has made was the launch of TON Strategy Co. in August, a new division backed by a heavily oversubscribed $558 million private placement. The initiative positions Verb as a treasury vehicle for Toncoin, the native cryptocurrency of the TON blockchain. We will discuss this in more detail later.

Financial Review

I first want to review the financials of Verb and see if there is any value in the company’s operations without any discussion on the treasure strategy. Verb’s financial trajectory has been volatile over the years with the business changes. In fiscal year 2024, revenue increased to $895,000, a more than 13x increase from the prior year. Although the amount of revenue it started with was nearly nothing. Revenues less than $1 million are not something to brag about. The momentum accelerated into 2025: first-quarter revenue reached $1.3 million, already surpassing the entire revenue base of 2024. The second quarter saw even higher revenues of $2.1 million. This growth in revenue has primarily come from Go Fund Yourself. The show launched in July 2024 and has started to make significant contributions to revenue. Market.Live has also seen its revenues increase. The table below outlines the revenues for each business unit and the company as a whole.

Revenue from Go Fund Yourself has grown significantly since launch and has far surpassed MARKET.Live in revenue. My question is: how much growth can the company have from one show, and how sustainable is that growth? Yes, it is showing really good growth and still has a lot of room to run. Revenues of $1.4 million are not that large, and I would expect further growth there. The show could lose popularity, and revenues could decrease at any time. I don’t know if revenues from 1 TV show are a sustainable business. In the long term, I just don’t see this as a sustainable source of growth. The company will likely have to create new shows in the future and hope they stick with the audience.

MARKET.Live is also seeing good growth. Revenues increased 22% quarter over quarter. The only problem is that it is coming off a small revenue base. Those growth rates will have to continue for a while before they are able to push revenues high enough to break even. The company is currently losing more money than it is generating in revenue. That means it needs large growth for years to come before it has a chance to get to profitability.

The company ended Q2 with over $10 million in cash or equivalents on the balance sheet. The company burned through $3.35 million during the quarter. I would expect that rate to decrease as revenues continue to grow. That being said, the company only has 3 quarters of a runway at the current burn rate.

TON Strategy

The main cause for the recent stock spike is due to the news that Verb raised $558 million to purchase TON as its primary Treasury reserve asset. This news caused the stock price to jump from $9.51 a share to over $20 in a trading day.

The company noted the following its TON Strategy:

“The company's strategy targets the accumulation of over 5% of $TON’s circulating supply, establishing the Company as a significant participant in maintaining and securing the network infrastructure. In addition, the Company intends to steadily increase $TON held per share through reinvestment of cash flows, staking rewards, and disciplined capital markets activity… The strategy is expected to generate sustainable staking rewards to initiate, manage, and grow the company's $TON exposure in a cash flow-positive manner. The Company intends to rebrand as TON Strategy Co. (TSC) to reflect its new direction.”

So what are TON and Toncoin? From Wikipedia:

“TON, also known as The Open Network (previously Telegram Open Network), is a decentralized layer-1 blockchain. TON was originally developed by Nikolai Durov, who is also known for his role in creating the messaging platform Telegram.

Telegram had planned to use TON to launch its own cryptocurrency (Gram), but was forced to abandon the project in 2020 following an injunction by US regulators. The network was then renamed, and independent developers have created their own cryptocurrencies and decentralized applications (dApps) using TON. Toncoin, the principal token of The Open Network, is deeply integrated into the Telegram messaging app, used for paying rewards to creators and developers, buying Telegram ads, hosting giveaways, or purchasing services such as Telegram Premium.”

Telegram has nearly one billion monthly active users. It is easy to see why TON represents one of the fastest-growing blockchain ecosystems. Verb aims to leverage this integration by building one of the largest publicly traded Toncoin treasuries in existence.

Outlook and Growth Catalysts

Verb’s growth prospects are tied to livestream commerce. I expect the global livestream shopping market to continue to grow, driven by social media integration and influencer-led retail. MARKET.live and Lyvecom provide Verb with direct exposure to this trend. Verb is a very small player in this market with revenues of $688 thousand during the quarter. I do not know if it will ever be able to establish itself as a dominant player in the market. Go Fund Yourself is also in the same vein as livestream commerce. The company is seeing strength in the show, but I see limitations on the long-term growth potential.

The TON Strategy initiative adds a new growth driver. Verb effectively holds an asset-backed growth model tied to an expanding blockchain ecosystem. This provides shareholders with both operational upside and exposure to crypto market appreciation. If TON adoption within the Telegram ecosystem accelerates as expected, Verb could benefit from both Treasury appreciation and strategic positioning as a publicly traded TON proxy.

Price and Value

At roughly $20 per share, Verb’s market capitalization hovers around $1.25 billion. The company announced that it had $780 million in the Treasury, with $713 million being in TON. If we take that off the market cap, we are left with a market cap of nearly half a billion. I have a hard time justifying that valuation with an estimated annual revenue of $5.5 million and an operating loss for the year. The company would have to grow revenues at a very high rate for many years to grow into that valuation.

Much of the value is coming from the potential growth of their treasury strategy and the growth of TON. If an investor thinks that TON will appreciate its price, they would be better off buying it directly and not being tied to the loss-making entity.

Risks and Headwinds

There are a lot of risks for the company. Execution risk looms large. MARKET.live has shown promising growth, but revenues remain in the low single millions. Scaling this to a level that can justify its valuation and sustain long-term profitability will require significant adoption by merchants and influencers, and the competitive landscape is crowded with well-funded rivals such as TikTok Shop and Amazon Live.

The TON Strategy Co. pivot introduces heavy reliance on cryptocurrency markets. While Toncoin has shown rapid adoption, it remains volatile and subject to both speculative cycles and regulatory uncertainty.

Finally, shareholder dilution remains a risk. While the $558 million financing provides ample capital, further capital raises, acquisitions, or share issuances could impact existing shareholder value.

Investment Verdict

I don’t see the operating business being near the value assigned to it. The company is showing strong growth rates, mostly related to its Go Fund Yourself business unit. I think there is continued room for growth within the space. I also think of the MARKET.Live operates in a growing market. The company is just far away from any real profits and value, with revenues expected at $5.5 million for the year.

If an investor wants exposure to TON, then I would suggest that the investor go and purchase the crypto directly rather than gain exposure through a loss-making entity. The company will have to supplement its loss-making operations with any staking profits from TON. Better to buy the crypto and reinvest any staking rewards. It is not available on all the large exchanges due to its investigation by the SEC, but it is available for purchase on some major exchanges (Kraken).

I am not endorsing the purchase of TON or doing an evaluation on the crypto. Obviously, the value of Verb is tied to the crypto at this point, but if you think the future of Verb is bright because of TON, then I think you would be better served purchasing the crypto directly. For this reason, I do not recommend purchasing the stock. The stock very well may see a price increase due to the appreciation of TON, but that return would be better if you owned the coin directly.
