data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: Ample Reserves, Dividends, Growth, And Repurchase Program, Despite Oil Price Headwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Canadian Natural Resources (NYSE:CNQ) a buy rating. I am of the opinion that the company will continue to create value for investors. This year, CNQ expects to increase its production by 7.90%. In addition, since at least 2016, its production has consistently grown year-over-year. CNQ has repurchased its common shares. From March of this year to March 2026, it expects to buy back up to 10% of its outstanding common shares. Furthermore, the company pays dividends. From 2020 to the present, it has paid CA$12.31 per share in dividends. To grant context, at the end of 2023, CNQ's stock price was CA$21.00 per share.

CNQ still has the potential to perform well. As of 2024, it had a proved reserve life index of 33 years. In addition, it has reported positive earnings in seven of the last eight years, indicating that it is a profitable firm.

However, lower oil prices could undermine the company's financial performance. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) projects that Brent crude oil will average $51 per barrel in 2026. In addition, the high debt level is another risk factor. The company had a 51.57% liabilities-to-assets ratio in Q2 2025, a higher ratio than those reported by other Canadian companies. Finally, Canadian green policies could be a hurdle to CNQ's production. Nonetheless, when pondering all positive and negative aspects of CNQ, I am still of the opinion that the company is an excellent investment opportunity.

Motivation

In April of this year, I wrote my first article on CNQ. Since that day, the company's stock price has increased by only 0.47%. Despite the low return, I still believe that CNQ represents an interesting investment opportunity. The critical point is to be patient and wait until the investment generates results.

Companies have different ways to create value for shareholders. The first one is to reinvest its resources to expand its operations. With this strategy, companies will increase their revenues, generating higher earnings. The second option is to pay dividends to its investors. Another alternative is to implement a repurchase program, reducing its common shares. In the following three sections, I will discuss how CNQ has created value for its investors through these three methods.

Production

CNQ has expanded its production. In 2017, its oil production was 962.26 thousand barrels of oil equivalent per day (Mboe/d), increasing to 1,420.36 Mboe/d in Q2 2025, for a 47.61% growth, as depicted in the following chart. The company has increased its production year-over-year. Even in 2020, a year in which the oil market was affected by COVID-19, CNQ expanded its oil and gas output.

For this year, the company expects to increase its production to 1,532.5 Mboe/d, the midpoint of its guidance, representing a 7.90% gain on a yearly basis. To accomplish this goal, it will invest CA$6,050 million in capital expenditures (CapEx). Furthermore, in July of this year, the company acquired assets in Montney, Canada, that were producing 32 Mboe/d. This acquisition will boost CNQ's production even further.

As is to be expected, the revenues of CNQ have increased due to the higher volume of oil and gas sold by the company. Its revenues were CA$21,027 million in 2018, reaching CA$35,656 million in 2024. Meanwhile, the net income grew to CA$6,106 million in 2024, for a 135.66% growth compared to 2018. To provide context, the annual average of the WTI oil price only increased by 17.48% in the same period.

The higher volume of production of CNQ has caused a positive effect on its stock price. In fact, the company's stock price has increased by 197.96% over a five-year period, as depicted in the following chart. As such, investors have benefited as a result of higher production levels.

Common Shares

The company has consistently repurchased its common shares. From 2018 to Q2 2025, its weighted average common shares outstanding have declined by 12.13%, as depicted in the following chart. At first glance, this might not seem to be a significant reduction. However, CNQ's strategy of buying back its common shares has created value for investors.

In March of this year, the company approved a repurchase program. It expects to buy up to 10% of its common shares between March 13, 2025, and March 12, 2026. This decision will have two positive effects for shareholders. The first is that investors' CNQ common shares could increase by up to 10%. In addition, it will reduce the supply of CNQ's common shares, pushing the company's stock price up.

Dividends

Another way to create value for investors is through dividends. From 2020 to now, CNQ has paid CA$12.31 per share in dividends. To provide context, at the end of 2019, CNQ's stock price was CA$16.18. As such, those investors who bought shares in CNQ that day and held the position until the present would have received an equivalent of 76.10% of the purchase price in dividends.

Proved Reserves

In 2024, CNQ has 15.2 billion boe of proved reserves. If the company maintains its oil and gas production at the level of 2024, its proved reserves will last for 33 years. Consequently, the company can extract oil for years without acquiring new lands. CNQ's proved reserves include oil and gas. For instance, the U.S. crude oil and lease condensate proved reserves were 46.4 billion barrels in 2023.

To add another comparison, Warren Buffett, the famous investor, indicated that the ample fossil reserves of Occidental Petroleum (OXY) were one of the reasons that led Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) to invest in that oil company. As of 2024, Occidental Petroleum had 4.6 billion boe of proved reserves and a proved reserve life index of 8.61 years, considerably lower than that of CNQ. As such, investments in CNQ are even more profitable.

Profit Margin

CNQ has a high profit margin, which is a competitive advantage. It has achieved positive earnings in seven of the last eight years, as illustrated in the following chart. So far, in the second quarter of this year, it reported a 28.27% net income margin. It only reported a net loss of 2.58% in 2020, which is not a significant loss. That year, COVID-19 negatively impacted the global oil demand. In fact, the WTI oil price averaged $39.16 per barrel. Therefore, even in a very adverse year, the company was not severely impacted.

Low Declining Assets

CNQ has low declining assets. This means that the company can extract oil from its assets for years without those assets depleting. In fact, the current CNQ's production decline rate is 11%. This implies that the company does not have to invest significant resources in capital expenditure to keep up its production. On the other hand, fracking companies require substantial investments in capex to maintain their production levels.

To illustrate the advantage that CNQ has compared to oil and gas fracking companies, the following table shows that CNQ's depreciation and amortization (D&A) expenses only represent 18.15% of the company's revenues. I obtained this ratio by subtracting the company's EBITDA margin from its EBIT margin. Meanwhile, OXY, Diamondback Energy (FANG), Permian Resources (PR), Matador Resources (MTDR), and EOG Resources (EOG) have higher D&A-to-revenues ratios than CNQ. Excluding CNQ, the other firms are oil fracking companies.

For instance, FANG has a 31.77% D&A-to-revenue ratio. This ratio indicates that the company has to invest significant resources to keep up its production. If FANG does not invest substantial resources in capex, its production will experience a significant decline in one year. In contrast, CNQ could reduce its capex, and its production will not be severely affected.

Trans Mountain Pipeline

In May 2024, the expansion of the Trans Mountain Pipeline began its commercial operations. This pipeline transports oil from Edmonton, Alberta, Canada, to the Pacific coast of that country. With the expansion, the pipeline increased its daily capacity from 300,000 barrels of oil per day to 890,000 barrels of oil per day. As such, Canadian oil has more access to the international market, improving the price paid per barrel of oil. Without this infrastructure, CNQ would have to sell its oil production at a significant discount compared to the WTI.

In addition, Canada has increased its oil exports to the U.S. Gulf Coast. For instance, between 2013 and 2023, Canadian oil exports to the U.S. Gulf Coast increased by 505%. This growth has fueled demand for Canadian oil.

The expansion of the Trans Mountain Pipeline and higher exports of Canadian oil to the U.S. Gulf Coast have contributed to a decline in the WTI-to-WCS ratio (WCS is the Canadian oil price benchmark). In fact, in the first six months of this year, this ratio averaged 1.22, declining to values not seen since 2011, as depicted in the following chart. In June of this year, the ratio decreased to 1.17. A lower ratio is better for Canadian oil producers, since these firms will sell their production at a price closer to the WTI oil price. Ultimately, CNQ's revenues and earnings will be positively affected.

Debt Risk

I think that debt is a negative aspect of CNQ. In Q2 2025, the company had a 51.57% liabilities-to-assets ratio, as depicted in the following chart. This ratio indicates that more than half of the company is composed of debt. If energy prices decline, the company could experience financial difficulties in paying its obligations.

This section will be incomplete without including other Canadian oil and gas companies in the analysis. The following table shows the debt-to-equity and current ratios of CNQ, Cenovus Energy (CVE), Suncor (SU), Imperial Oil Limited (IMO), Baytex Energy (BTE), MEG Energy (OTCPK:MEGEF), Obsidian Energy (OBE), Athabasca Oil (OTCPK:ATHOF), and Tourmaline Oil (OTCPK:TRMLF). CNQ has the second-highest debt-to-equity ratio, a negative aspect. In addition, it has the third-lowest current ratio. These figures indicate that CNQ relies on debt to fund its operations.

Carbon Capture

Several hurdles affect the oil production in Canada. The Canadian green policy is one of them. There is a proposal to limit the oil and gas greenhouse gas emissions in Canada. This proposal aims to cap oil and gas emissions for the first compliance period, 2030-2032, at 27% below the emissions that will be reported in 2026.

CNQ has implemented initiatives to reduce its gas emissions. The company has a facility that can capture 2.7 million tonnes of CO2 per year. This year, it plans to invest CA$90 million in carbon capture. However, this facility is insufficient to reduce the company's oil and gas emissions significantly.

For instance, in 2023, the company said that it expects to reduce its gas emissions by 22 megatonnes, or 22 million tonnes, per year by 2030. Consequently, CNQ has achieved only 12.27% of its plan capture program. If the Canadian government restricts the oil and gas greenhouse emissions, CNQ will have to invest significant resources to reduce its emissions.

Price Risk

Oil prices have declined by 30.51% over the last three years. The WTI oil price trades at $62.52 per barrel. For 2026, the EIA projects that Brent crude oil will average $51 per barrel. This crude oil generally trades at a higher price than the WTI oil price. If oil prices continue to decline, CNQ revenues and net income will be negatively impacted.

Peer Analysis

This section conducts a peer analysis among some of the most relevant oil and gas producers in Canada. To implement the comparison, I selected the following companies: CNQ, CVE, SU,IMO, BTE, MEGEF, OBE, ATHOF, and TRMLF. The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

In the last year, the CNQ's stock price declined by 14.33%, a further drop than the median value. In addition, over a five-year period, its stock price increased by 282.79%, lower than the median value. Both figures indicate that CNQ has underperformed among its peers.

However, the company is expected to pay a dividend yield of 5.71%, the second-highest value among its peers. In addition, it paid, on average, a dividend yield of 4.66% in the last four years, higher than the median ratio, which is a positive aspect.

Moving to the valuation metrics, CNQ has higher EV/EBITDA, price-to-book, and price-to-cash flow ratios than the median of these ratios, indicating that the company is overvalued compared to its peers.

On the other hand, the company's revenues are expected to decline by 1.53% this year. In addition, over a five-year period, the revenues increased at a compound annual growth rate of 14.35%. Both ratios are lower than their respective medians. Therefore, CNQ has a worse revenue performance compared to the median ratios.

Finally, the profitability ratios indicate that CNQ is more profitable than the median ratios in the peer group. CNQ has a net income margin of 21.90%, considerably higher than the median ratio. Further, it has the highest unleveraged FCF margin with 17.48%.

Overall, the company has seven ratios with better results than their respective medians. It also has seven ratios with worse results than their respective medians. However, it has better results in key metrics, such as profit margins. In addition, its revenues have grown over the last five years.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I think that CNQ represents an excellent investment opportunity. The key aspect is the long term. The company's stock price could decline in the short term. However, in the long term, CNQ's stock price will likely increase.

To begin with, the company repurchases its common shares. Consequently, investors will have more common shares over time. Furthermore, its oil and natural gas production grew by 47.61% from 2017 to Q2 2025. Meanwhile, for this year, it plans to expand its production by 7.90% compared to 2024. CNQ achieved these results thanks to its profit margin and its ample oil reserves.

THE EIA projects lower oil prices for 2026, a risk factor for CNQ. In addition, in Q2 2025, the company had a 51.57% liabilities-to-assets ratio, indicating that more than half of the company's assets are composed of debt. Furthermore, Canadian green policies could limit CNQ's oil production. Ultimately, when taking all of these aspects into consideration, I still consider CNQ to be an excellent investment opportunity.

data.title: Where Apartment Rents May Accelerate Soonest
data.description:
By Mike Sobolik, Investment Strategist, Direct Real Estate, North America

Introduction

Market conditions have been shifting in the U.S. apartment sector, one of the most highly represented property types in institutional real estate.1 U.S. apartment construction starts for the year ending 2Q 2025 fell to their lowest level since 2012, while tenant demand reached a record high.2 This imbalance suggests that many apartment markets could see vacancies tighten, potentially supporting significant rent growth over the next one to two years.

Rent growth has long been a key driver of apartment value growth. Over the past 20 years, the correlation between annual effective rent growth3 and annual property appreciation has been very strong at 0.80.4

The importance of rent growth becomes more pronounced when compared at the submarket level. From 1Q-2000 to 2Q-2025, the average five-year rent growth spread between top- and bottom-quartile submarkets was 4.8 percentage points (lowest: 3.2 points). This wide gap highlights the opportunity to outperform by selecting submarkets with stronger rent growth potential.

We have developed a framework to identify which markets/submarkets we believe will be best positioned to drive outsized rent growth the soonest. This paper walks through a simplified version of this framework.

Methodology for projecting high rent growth submarkets

  • We focused on U.S. metropolitan areas with at least 100,000 apartment units.5 (53 metro areas; 723 submarkets).
  • We then identified metro-level factors most strongly correlated with effective rent growth.
  • Occupancy rates, the factor most highly correlated with metro rent growth, was projected for submarkets based on current occupancy/vacancy, units currently under construction, and trailing one-year demand levels.
  • We used those projections to find submarkets most likely in the coming year to surpass 96% occupancy, the point at which rent growth tends to speed up. (494 of 723 met this criterion)
  • These were narrowed further to submarkets in metros where 75%+ of inventory is projected to exceed 96% critical occupancy (260 submarkets).
  • Finally, these 260 submarkets were differentiated by metro job growth and metro cap rates6 to assess investment tradeoffs.

Key drivers of rent growth

Among the 53 metros analyzed, occupancy rates and job growth were most consistently correlated with effective rent growth7:

  • Occupancy rate correlation average: 0.72 (standard deviation8: 0.09)
  • Job growth correlation average: 0.61 (standard deviation: 0.11)

Other tested factors—demand growth, supply growth, construction starts, units under construction, and Baa bond rates9—showed lower average correlations and less consistent correlations across metros (i.e., higher standard deviations).

Occupancy data is available at the submarket level, while job growth is only available at the metro level. Therefore, occupancy was used first to filter submarkets, with job growth applied later.

Critical occupancy level

Rent growth aggregated to the national level tends to accelerate when national average occupancy exceeds 96%. Although this threshold may not apply uniformly across submarkets, it serves as a useful guide for filtering submarkets.

Nationally, rent growth tends to increase when occupancy exceeds 96%

U.S. apartment occupancy and rent growth (%)

1Q-2000 to 2Q-2025

Submarket projections

Next, we applied the 96% occupancy threshold to current conditions in all 723 submarkets:

  • Total availability was estimated by combining 2Q 2025 vacancies with units under construction.
  • We then compared total availability to two demand scenarios:
    • Demand continues at the pace of the past year (which was at or near historical highs in many areas due to higher mortgage rates weakening affordability).
    • Demand reverts to the trailing 10-year average.

Under the first scenario, 494 submarkets (68%) are projected to reach or exceed 96% occupancy by mid-2026. If demand reverts to the 10-year average, this number drops significantly. However, we believe, strained homeownership affordability supports the likelihood of continued strong apartment demand.

Critical mass metros

The 494 submarkets projected to reach 96% occupancy in the next year are spread across all 53 metros. Some metros have many such submarkets; others have only one.

We believe submarkets surrounded by several high-occupancy, low-supply areas are more likely to sustain stronger rent growth. Narrowing our focus to metros where 75%+ of inventory are in submarkets projected to reach critical occupancy in the next year (what we’ll call “critical mass metros”) results in 260 submarkets in 23 metros.

Submarkets on pace to reach critical occupancy within critical mass metros

260 out of 723 submarkets

Investment tradeoffs

The goal of this analysis goes beyond identifying submarkets with the greatest likelihood of significant rent growth. It’s to find submarkets with the best prospects for total return performance potential. For that reason, we analyzed the 260 submarkets using:

  1. Metro job growth: Strongly correlated with metro effective rent growth.
  2. Cap rates: Reflects the price investors pay for property income growth.

Comparing these 260 submarkets with metro job growth in 2024 and cap rates reported in 2Q 2025 provides perspective about potential investment tradeoffs:

  • High job growth, high cap rate submarkets (15%, or 39 submarkets) - Best positioned for stronger rent growth at attractive pricing.
  • Low job growth, high cap rate submarkets (48%, or 125 submarkets) - This category accounts for nearly half of the 260 submarkets. Must assess whether higher cap rates offset the risks of slower demand growth.
  • High job growth, low cap rate submarkets (5%, or 12 submarkets) - Investors are paying a premium for expected growth. This requires high confidence in the strength and sustainability of job growth.
  • Low job growth and low cap rate submarkets (6%, or 15 submarkets) - Risk of overpaying in slower job growth metros despite high occupancy.

This simple framework is useful but has limitations.

  • 2024 job growth may not reflect future trends. A well-rounded framework would examine more than one reasonable scenario for future job growth.
  • Metro-level cap rates can differ from property-level rates.
  • Comparative submarket strength can vary widely due to differences in performance drivers, such as the growth posture of local employers; current and changing local housing regulations; gaps between the local cost of homeownership versus renting; the degree of local availability of housing alternatives. None of these factors are explicitly part of this analysis but should be factored when comparing potential submarket strength.

Still, this framework provides a useful starting point for selecting submarkets.

Submarkets on pace to reach critical occupancy within critical mass metros

One-year critical occupancy submarkets by relative metro cap rate and metro job growth

260 out of 723 submarkets

Regional differences

Going back to the 494 submarkets expected to reach or exceed 96% occupancy within the next year, clear regional patterns emerge:

  • Northeast: Most submarkets already exceed the 96% critical occupancy rate.
  • Midwest and West: Increasing number of critical occupancy submarkets.
  • South: Usually posts the strongest job growth yet also contains the largest supply pipeline at present; slower path to critical occupancy.

Northeast and Midwest have higher percentages of critical occupancy submarkets

Percent of submarkets per region exceeding or approaching critical occupancy rate of 96%

Conclusion: Use data to improve performance probability

Understanding key drivers — occupancy rates, job growth, and cap rates—can improve investment decisions about apartment submarket locations. Although no framework guarantees perfect outcomes, this kind of approach may improve the probability of achieving better relative performance potential.

Defined Terms and Notes:

  1. Source: NCREIF Property Index (NPI) as of July 2025. NCREIF Property Index (NPI) is published by the National Council of Real Estate Investment Fiduciaries (NCREIF). NPI is a quarterly, unleveraged composite total return for private commercial real estate properties held for investment purposes only. All properties in the NPI have been acquired, at least in part, on behalf of tax-exempt institutional investors and held in a fiduciary environment. In 2Q-2025, the apartment sector represented 29.7% of total market value in the NPI. There may be material differences in the investment goals, liquidity needs, and investment horizons of individual and institutional investors. Investors should consult with a financial professional regarding their own situation and risk tolerance before making any investment decisions.
  2. Source: RealPage as of July 2025. Annual U.S. apartment construction starts at the end of 2Q-2025 totaled 215,195 units, the lowest level since 1Q-2012 at 196,156 units. Annual tenant demand for the year ending 2Q-2025 totaled 794,160 units, the highest level since RealPage began to keep records in 2000.
  3. Effective rent growth is the percent change in average effective rent per unit between two time periods. Effective rent represents the actual rent level at which units on average are rented after offerings of free rent and other concessions are deducted from the advertised rent level (typically referred to as “asking rent”).
  4. Source: RealPage and NCREIF as of July 2025. Correlations between year-over year-growth of effective rents and indexed property values from the NCREIF Property Index were calculated from 3Q-2005 to 2Q-2025. Correlation indicates the degree to which two investments have historically moved in the same direction and magnitude.
  5. Apartment data used in this report is sourced from Real Page as of July 2025.
  6. Capitalization rates (cap rates) is the quotient of a property’s net operating income divided by the property’s estimate value.
  7. Data for this analysis reflect conditions between the periods of 1Q-2000 to 2Q-2025.
  8. Standard Deviation is a statistic that measures the dispersion of a dataset relative to its mean.
  9. A credit rating is an assessment provided by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO) of the creditworthiness of an issuer with respect to debt obligations, including specific securities, money market instruments or other debts. Ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest); ratings are subject to change without notice. NR indicates the debtor was not rated and should not be interpreted as indicating low quality. For more information on rating methodologies, please visit the following NRSRO websites: www.standardandpoors.com and select 'Understanding Credit Ratings' under Rating Resources 'About Ratings' on the homepage.; https://ratings.moodys.io/ratings and select 'Understanding Ratings' on the homepage.; www.fitchratings.com and select 'Ratings Definitions Criteria' under 'Resources' on the homepage. Then select 'Rating Definitions' under 'Resources' on the 'Contents' menu.

Investment risks

The value of investments and any income will fluctuate (this may partly be the result of exchange rate fluctuations) and investors may not get back the full amount invested. Property and land can be difficult to sell, so investors may not be able to sell such investments when they want to. The value of property is generally a matter of an independent valuer’s opinion and may not be realised.

Generally, real estate assets are illiquid in nature. Although certain kinds of investments are expected to generate current income, the return of capital and the realization of gains, if any, from an investment will often occur upon the partial or complete disposition of such investment.

Investing in real estate typically involves a moderate to high degree of risk. The possibility of partial or total loss of capital will exist. Investing in commercial real estate assets involves certain risks, including but not limited to: tenants' inability to pay rent; increases in interest rates and lack of availability of financing; tenant turnover and vacancies; and changes in supply of or demand for similar property types in a given market.

Important information

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions if they are uncertain whether an investment is suitable for them.

Data as of August 2025, unless otherwise stated.

Where individuals or the business have expressed opinions, they are based on current market conditions, they may differ from those of other investment professionals and are subject to change without notice.

Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions, there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.
